content.full_text:
Mary L. Leach
527 South L Street
Lompoc, CA 93436

February 28, 1990

Kathryn E. Donovan, General Council
Fair Political Practice Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Request for confirmation of telephone advice

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This is a request for confirmation of telephone advice I received
from Ms. Blanca Breeze at the FPPC on February 27, 1990. At that
time I explained to Ms. Breeze that I was recently appointed a
Planning Commissioner for the City of Lompoc. Prior to my appointment, as a citizen of Lompoc, I spoke out against a re-zone
application by former Planning Commission Chairman, Mr. Michael
Knoell. I spoke out against this re-zone at a public hearing
before the City Council and in the local newspaper. Although I
did turn in a petition with approximately 30 signatures on it, at
the Council I stated for the record I was speaking for myself and
my husband.

I was not compensated in any way for my voice. I have a small
collection consultant business but none of the opponents who have
spoken out against this re-zone are clients of mine. At least
95% of my clients live out of town.

On December 5, 1989 the City Council of Lompoc approved a re-zone
for Michael Knoell at 410 South H Street in Lompoc. The
developer had requested his 10,500 sq. ft. lot in the middle of
the block in an R-2 zone be re-zoned PD (Planned Development) so
that he might divide the lot into three lots to be sold independently On or about December 17, 1989 the City suspended the
developer's permission on a third building proposed for the lot,
which was already developed with two homes. On January 2, 1990
the opponents of the re-zone, myself included, gave testimony to
the city Council in the belief that the staff had misled the
council, advising that such a re-zone approval would set a precedence not in the best interest of the neighborhood or the city
as a whole, and suggesting that a few ordinances had been misinterpreted in the developer's favor.

The City Council initiated a moratorium on all R-2 building,
sending the re-zone back to the Planning Commission and
requesting that the ordinances be reviewed and clarified so that
each ordinance would be applied equally to all developers.

(Page 1 of 3)

A few weeks later I was appointed to the Planning Commission.
That same night the Council amended the moratorium to include R-3
development.

On February 13, 1990 the Planning Commission was scheduled to
have a discussion regarding the issues related to the R-2, R-3
moratorium. On the afternoon of Feb. 13 the City Attorney asked
to speak with me, stated that he had received an inquiry about my bias
in this case and suggested I might have a conflict of interest.
He briefly mentioned a monetary clause but said first he must
have staff check the radius of my property from the proposed lot
re-zone. He also asked me if I owned R-2 property stating that
would definitely be a conflict of interest. I do not.

My home is within a 2500 ft. radius of the proposed re-zone but
it is in an R-1-10 zone. This re-zone would not impact my neigh-
borhood or affect its value any more than it would impact or
affect the value of any home in the city.

I explained to Ms. Breeze that I felt singled out. I could not
understand how my conflict was any greater than another
commissioner who had previously voted on this issue and also
lived within 2500 ft. of the property, or a third who had voted
before and owned R-2 and R-3 property in the city. I have
nothing to gain monetarily from my opposition.

On the evening of Feb. 13 the Staff and Planning Commission chose
to change the ordinances in the developer's favor rather than
clarify them since they believed it was not important what they
were but what they should be. I objected to this action. I
believed the Commission had strayed from the Council's objective
to clarify the laws as they existed.

On February 27, 1990 the text amendment recommendation to the
council came up for vote again. Since the text amendments
involved the very issues which were raised at the Knoell re-zone
the City Attorney felt that bias could be implied and suggested I
not vote even though this decision was legislative not quasi-
judicial and would affect me and the public equality as much as it
affected the developer.

In addition, on February 27th the re-zone application came before
the commission again as a totally separate agenda item. The City
Attorney felt that since I was "embroiled in" the controversy
that bias could be implied and therefore I should not vote on the
re-zone to avoid the "appearance of impropriety." He also
recommended I contact the FPPC.

Ms. Breeze explained to me that I have no conflict of interest
since I have no monetary stake; that my First Amendment rights
give me the right to speak and that I was appointed specifically
for my voice. In addition, she explained that everyone comes to
a public seat with some bias and therefore my bias was no greater
than any other.

(Page 2 of 3)

Please send me written confirmation of the telephone advice given
by Blanca Breeze on February 27, 1990. If you have any question
regarding the above information don't hesitate to call me at
(805) 736-3954. Thank you for your time and attention to this
matter.

Respectfully Yours,

Mary Leach
Mary Leach
Planning Commissioner
City of Lompoc, California

(Page 3 of 3)

sections.question_synthetic: Does a newly appointed Planning Commissioner have a conflict of interest under FPPC rules if she previously spoke as a citizen against a re-zone application and now must vote on related matters, given that she has no financial stake and lives in a different zone?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a member of the public asking for written confirmation of prior telephone advice. The document lacks formal Q&A sections but contains substantial factual narrative about the applicant's situation. No FPPC response/conclusion is present in this document. The telephone advice is summarized at the end of page 2, where Ms. Breeze allegedly advised no conflict exists. Date discrepancy noted: letter dated February 28, 1990 but references telephone call on February 27, 1990.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Does a newly appointed Planning Commissioner have a conflict of interest under FPPC rules if she previously spoke as a citizen against a re-zone application and now must vote on related matters, given that she has no financial stake and lives in a different zone?

embedding.summary: Mary Leach, a newly appointed Planning Commissioner for Lompoc, requests written confirmation of telephone advice from FPPC staff (Ms. Blanca Breeze) regarding whether she has a conflict of interest in voting on matters related to a re-zone application she previously opposed as a citizen.
